skill.name: mcp-server
skill.description: Guide for designing, implementing, testing, and evaluating Model Context Protocol servers in Python or TypeScript.
skill.summary: Build agent-friendly MCP servers with structured tools, clear schemas, robust error handling, and evaluation workflows.
skill.author: Cam10001110101

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-severity matches, but most are false positives from markdown examples, Apache License text, naming guidance, and reference checklists. The confirmed risk is the included evaluation harness: it can launch user-specified stdio commands, pass environment variables, write reports, and connect to user-specified remote MCP endpoints. No evidence of prompt injection, obfuscation, or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Evaluation Harness Executes User-Specified MCP Server Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The evaluation script accepts a stdio command and arguments from the CLI, then passes them to the MCP stdio connection helper. This is legitimate for an MCP evaluator, but running untrusted commands can execute arbitrary local code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution path is clear and intentional through stdio MCP server startup. The risk depends on user-supplied command trust, so this is elevated but not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Remote MCP Endpoint and Header Handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The evaluation harness supports SSE and HTTP URLs plus custom headers. This can expose credentials or task data to a user-selected remote MCP endpoint if reviewers use untrusted URLs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly routes user-provided URLs and headers into MCP network clients. The behavior is expected for testing remote servers, but it requires user caution.

medium_findings.title: Secret and Environment Variable Forwarding
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The harness parses KEY=VALUE environment variables and passes them to the stdio server process. This is useful for tokens, but secrets can be exposed to any command selected by the user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The env parsing and forwarding logic is direct. This is normal for local MCP testing, but sensitive variables must only be passed to trusted processes.

low_findings.title: Documentation and License Static Matches Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Many weak cryptography, hardcoded URL, and command execution matches occur in Apache License text, markdown guidance, code examples, and checklists rather than hidden executable payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are prose, examples, or standard license text. They do not show covert execution or credential exfiltration intent.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Indicators Not Found
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for prompt-injection concerns. Targeted review found no suspicious override text, claimed system authority, pre-approval claims, or instructions to skip analysis in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted search for known prompt injection phrases returned no matches. Confidence is high for the searched indicators but not absolute for every possible wording.

low_findings.title: Network and API Examples Are Intended Reference Material
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious networking. The markdown files include examples of HTTP clients, SDK documentation URLs, and placeholder API calls needed to teach MCP integration patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited network patterns are in instructional markdown examples or explicit documentation-fetch instructions. They align with the skill purpose and do not contain suspicious endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Command Launch
dangerous_patterns.description: The stdio evaluation path can start a command chosen by the user. Only trusted MCP server commands should be used.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly accepts command parameters and constructs stdio server parameters. This is expected but dangerous with untrusted inputs.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Remote Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The SSE and HTTP paths connect to a user-provided URL with optional headers. This can disclose data to untrusted MCP endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL and headers are passed into remote MCP clients. The feature is legitimate but sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Report File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The evaluation script writes reports to a user-provided output path. Users should avoid overwriting important files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write path is explicit and controlled by a CLI argument. It is a normal feature, with limited local filesystem risk.

content.value_statement: MCP servers are hard to design when tools simply mirror raw APIs. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through workflow design, implementation, testing, and evaluation.
actual_capabilities: Guides planning for agent-centered MCP tool workflows.
Explains Python FastMCP implementation patterns with Pydantic models.
Explains TypeScript MCP SDK patterns with Zod schemas.
Provides review checklists for naming, validation, pagination, and error handling.
Includes an evaluation harness for running XML question sets against MCP servers.
Documents safe testing approaches for long-running stdio MCP processes.
limitations: Does not build a complete production server without service-specific API details.
Evaluation scripts require local dependencies and an Anthropic API key.
Remote SSE or HTTP testing can contact user-supplied MCP endpoints.
Reference examples require human review before use with sensitive systems.

use_cases.title: Plan a new integration
use_cases.description: Identify high-value workflow tools before writing an MCP server for an external API.

use_cases.title: Implement server foundations
use_cases.description: Set up Python or TypeScript server structure with validation, errors, and response formatting.

use_cases.title: Evaluate tool quality
use_cases.description: Create read-only evaluation questions and run them through the included MCP evaluation harness.

prompt_templates.title: Choose MCP Tools
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new service integration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan an MCP server for [service]. List the core workflows, proposed tools, inputs, outputs, and error cases.

prompt_templates.title: Build Python Server
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a Python FastMCP implementation plan for [service]. Include Pydantic models, shared API helpers, and tool annotations.

prompt_templates.title: Review TypeScript Server
prompt_templates.scenario: Checking an existing TypeScript MCP server.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my TypeScript MCP server for schema quality, tool naming, async network handling, and actionable errors.

prompt_templates.title: Design Evaluation Suite
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design 10 read-only MCP evaluation questions for [server]. Each answer must be stable, verifiable, and require realistic tool use.

output_examples.input: Plan an MCP server for a project management API.
output: A workflow map covering tasks, comments, assignments, and status updates.
A prioritized tool list with concise names and clear input requirements.
Recommended response formats and pagination rules.

output_examples.input: Review an MCP server before release.
output: A checklist of schema, naming, validation, and error handling issues.
Specific recommendations for safer authentication and external API calls.
Suggested evaluation questions for read-only behavior testing.

output_examples.input: Create evaluations for a GitHub MCP server.
output: A set of realistic questions that require multiple read-only tool calls.
Stable expected answers suitable for automated comparison.
Guidance for running the included evaluation harness.

best_practices: Design tools around complete user workflows instead of raw API endpoints.
Use strict input validation, concise outputs, and actionable error messages.
Run evaluations only against trusted local commands and approved MCP endpoints.
anti_patterns: Do not expose broad tools that accept arbitrary unvalidated parameters.
Do not return large unfiltered API dumps when concise summaries are enough.
Do not pass secrets or headers to untrusted remote MCP servers.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build MCP servers that expose useful tools for AI agents and external services.

faq.question: Does it support Python and TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Python FastMCP guidance and TypeScript MCP SDK guidance.

faq.question: Does it include runnable scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes an evaluation harness that can connect to stdio, SSE, or HTTP MCP servers.

faq.question: Are the scripts safe to run automatically?
faq.answer: No. Review commands, URLs, headers, and environment variables before running the evaluation scripts.

faq.question: Can it evaluate destructive tools?
faq.answer: The evaluation guidance emphasizes read-only tasks and stable answers.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Developers and reviewers who create, test, or improve MCP integrations for AI coding tools.
